color_semantics.TEAL: quantum reality (wave amplitude, interference fringes, de Broglie wavelength, accumulated pattern)
color_semantics.CRIMSON: classical expectation (particle trajectory, two lumps, no fringes)

metadata.note: Card 04 — Tonomura 1989 single-electron double-slit; matter has a wavelength; one particle interferes with itself

beats.narration_text: One electron. One shot. No other electrons. And still — a wave interference pattern builds up, dot by dot.

card.headline: Why One Electron Interferes With Itself

beats.narration_text: Fire a stream of bullets at a wall with two slits. You get two lumps — one behind each slit. Bullets add up where they land. No interference.
beats.note: Classical: bullets through two slits → two CRIMSON lumps on detector

beats.narration_text: Fire electrons one at a time — never two at once. Each one hits the detector as a single point. No wave visible. How can there be fringes?

card.headline:
If each electron is a single particle,
where does the interference pattern come from?

beats.narration_text: A classical particle has a definite path. Through slit A or slit B — not both. Two detectors, zero interference. That is what classical physics predicts.
beats.note: Classical particle: trajectory arrows through either slit A or B; no fringes; CRIMSON two-lump pattern

beats.narration_text: Each electron is also a wave. Louis de Broglie: matter has a wavelength, λ = h/p. The electron's probability amplitude passes through both slits simultaneously — and the two wavefronts interfere.
beats.note: TEAL wave amplitude spreading from source, splitting at two slits, interfering, producing fringes

card.headline:
The amplitude passes through both slits.
Square it to get probability.

beats.narration_text: Where the wavefronts add up in phase — bright fringe. Where they cancel — dark fringe. The electron always lands at one point. But the probability of landing anywhere is shaped by the full interference of its own amplitude through both slits.
beats.note: Wave interference pattern on detector; bright and dark fringes labeled; single dot lands in bright region

beats.narration_text: After 100 electrons: random-looking dots. After 1,000: structure hints at fringes. After 70,000: a clear alternating pattern. Each individual dot was random. The pattern was not.
beats.note: Three screens: 100 dots (random), 1000 dots (hints), 70000 dots (clear fringes) — TEAL accumulation

card.headline: One electron — both slits at once

beats.narration_text: This was done. Tonomura, Endo, Matsuda, and Kawasaki at Hitachi in 1989. Electrons through a biprism, one at a time. 70,000 electrons later: a clean interference pattern. There is no classical reading of this.

props.headline: This was done. Tonomura, Endo, Matsuda, and Kawasaki at Hitachi in…

beats.note: STILL·ai: series of four detector screens accumulating dots, showing buildup from sparse dots to clear interference fringes

beats.narration_text: The electron's wave function passes through both slits. The probability of landing at each point is the squared amplitude of the interference. You can't watch which slit it 'really' went through — that question has no answer.
beats.note: Diagram showing wavefronts, both slits, interference; |ψ|² = fringe pattern; 'which slit?' crossed out CRIMSON

beats.narration_text: An electron at 50 keV has kinetic energy 50,000 eV. Momentum p = √(2mK). de Broglie wavelength λ = h/p ≈ 0.0055 nm — picometer scale. Fringe spacing for 300 nm slit separation: d sinθ = λ gives θ ≈ 0.001°. The pattern is tiny, but measurable.
beats.note: Calculation boxes: K=50 keV, p=√(2mK), λ=0.0055 nm; fringe spacing formula; TEAL labels

beats.narration_text: One electron, one shot, no partner. And still — both slits, interference, a pattern that only emerges when the wave and the probability are taken seriously. That is quantum mechanics.

card.headline:
The amplitude passes through both slits.
The pattern is the proof.

beats.name: OutroSeries
beats.narration_text: Part of the Quantum Mechanics A Companion Guide series.
